issues.title: Should we include an optional hash for stronger 1-way binding of certificates?
issues.author: johngray-dev
issues.body:
I was wondering if we should include an optional hash of the related certificate.   That would constrain issuance order (as the hash of one of them would need to be known), but it would give you the guarantee that you got the right certificate and that someone didn't compromise the URL location.    So it would be an optional stronger binding.

RelatedCertificateDescriptor ::= SEQUENCE {
	   uniformResourceIdentifier IA5String,
	   signatureAlgorithm 	[0] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
	   publicKeyAlgorithm 	[1] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
           hashAlgorithmId      [2] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
           certHash             [3] IMPLICIT OCTET STRING OPTIONAL 
   }

The hashAlgorithmId would be the hashAlgorithm used for certHash.   The certHash would be the hash of the related certificate using the specified hashAlgorithmId.   It is optional and would be a useful check if it is there and would offer a strong binding in one direction (which is still better than weak two-way binding).

Example UseCase...   PQ transition:    Issue ML-DSA certificate first with weak binding (no hash) to the location of a future traditional cert, then issue the Traditional RSA cert with this extension with the hash of the ML-DSA certificate.    Traditional software works fine.   When traditional software is upgraded the reference is used and hash verified.  If ML-DSA side needs to check traditional, it can still use the weaker (no hash) reference.   It could check that the traditional RSA has a reference including a hash of itself (but there would be no guarantee that a compromised traditional RSA certificate couldn't have just copied the public hash of the ML-DSA cert).   However this is not any weaker than what we are already proposing.

Alternatively, we could change certhash into TBSCertificate hash which could allow strong two-way binding, but then we would have to remove the RelatedCertificateDescriptor extension from each of the TBSCertificates first before computing the hash which would strongly couple issuance.   I don't think we want this one.  

So I think adding a hash in one direction is straight forward and gives it a stronger property.   I think most use-cases would only use it in one direction anyway.

comments.author: johngray-dev
johngray-dev

issues.title: Would a dataURI be allowed in RelatedCertificateDescriptor.uniformResourceIdentifier?
issues.author: ralienpp
issues.body:
Asking for a friend; would it be possible to use a [dataURI ](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2397.html)in the `uniformResourceIdentifier` field?

```
RelatedCertificateDescriptor ::= SEQUENCE { 
    uniformResourceIdentifier IA5String,    // here, something like `data:application/pkix-cert;base64,blablabla`
    signatureAlgorithm [0] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL, 
    publicKeyAlgorithm [1] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL 
}
```


Let's say, in my scenario I have limited connectivity options and it is not guaranteed that I'll be able to fetch the other certificate, so it would be nice if I get it right away on a platter.

comments.author: ounsworth
johngray-dev
comments.body:
Notes from May 13th, 2025:

Case for Upgrade - DCD in RSA
Statement of possession - 

Statement:  When this certificate is issued, it didn't provide a proof of possession.  No way to forward the proof.  Trusting the CA to make those statements.  

Side coversation:  Attestation results - evidence and directly device measured itself.  Endorsements.  3rd party says something about you.  Eg..  This device is FIPS certified....  Evidence and Endorsements get verified.  They know how to read the data format.  Command line tool, verified under some policy.  Attestation results.  Example Boolean (pass/fail).  Attestation results simple output of the verifier.  Into an X509Verifier.

Idea:
Use the URL to point to another CRL - no transport security - Use Issuer and SerialNumber.   Gets rid of Chicken and egg problem of back and fourth hashing. 
Problem:  Does Publishing the serial number in advance of signing reduces the entropy if it is made public.  

Open Research Question
 - Use IssuerandSerialNumber for stronger binding or maybe instead use URL of its own publishing location.  That is probably easier to reserve
 - Question:  Can we invent a new unique identifier field? - borrow from ACME?  full of URL's - eg. ACME/NewOrder/UUID 
 - SerialNumber+SKI? - Not supposed to be security relevant.  Use SKI as a kind of salt... 
 - UUID_Discovery = HASH(SerialNumber+SKI)   or HASH(IssuerAndSerial)to obfuscate the SerialNumber. 
 - UUID_DISCOVERY = HASH( .... some unique element(s) of cert ... )  
    - Look at ACME - commitment to a cert existing in the future (URL Scheme)
    - similar to how partitioned CRL's work (IDP checking)
    - URL is in the certificate itself - cert now has wrong DCD ID.  Have to check they match and do a full 5280 validation.
- Identifier for paired certificate - RFC 5697 draft
   - Check if precedence of Hash or build off of previous work - 
   - Check RFC 5697 (Other certificates extension) - section 4

issues.title: Create a python reference implementation
issues.author: johngray-dev
issues.body:
It would be great if we could create a reference implementation of certificate discovery in python that could be used as a reference. 

This would be a great hackathon project.

- Output of a couple of examples showing encoded certificates
  - one example with at least 2 SIA extensions showing different purposes

comments.author: johngray-dev
comments.body:
We should use examples of the following:

Dual Usage 
    -  Fully cross-link (By Uri) with a hash in one direction (ML-DSA / ML-KEM based certs)
    -  Fully cross-linked (By Uri) with RSA base (Dual Use sign and encryption), cross linked with (ML-DSA and another with ML-KEM).  Two hashes in the RSA.  
    -  By Inclusion (fully embedded cert - no hash needed)

Algorithm Migration
    - RSA Cert points to new (ML-DSA)  -  By Uri with Hash of RSA cert in the ML-DSA Cert.   With text like:   "In this use-case, you are trying to proove they are holding the correct RSA cert (by Hash inclusion).  You have to do a "back-check" to make sure the ML-DSA cert referenced the RSA cert.  To prevent encrypting data for the wrong certificate."

issues.title: Address Russ's comments on the new Draft
issues.author: johngray-dev
issues.body:
The ASN.1 Module in this draft does not compile.  There are many syntax problems and several things that need to be IMPORTed (like AlgorithmIdentifier, Certificate, and id-sha256).

I am also concerned about the use of "Purpose OBJECT IDENTIFIERs".  This seems too easy to confuse with EKU, which are also call key purpose identifiers.  Can we be more descriptive to avoid confusion?

issues.title: Comments from hackathon implentation
issues.author: ounsworth
issues.body:
Here's a random list of thoughts while implementing this at the interim hackathon.


# 3.1 / 3.2

ASN.1 says "byUri", and "uri IA5String".
Text says

> The certificate is referenced by an IA5String that contains the URI of the Secondary Certificate. The DER encoding of the Secondary Certificate MUST be available at the specified location.

To be water-tight, we probably either need a reference to an RFC that defines the syntax of a URI, or provide some examples and do a definition-by-example.

Also, I'm not sure I agree with that "MUST be available at the specified location". Right off the bat, I think all of our test vectors are going to violate that because we're not running a web server to host them. I suggest instead:

> The DER encoding of the Secondary Certificate SHOULD be available at the specified location, but in cases where the certificates are used in an offline environment, the `uri` MAY be used as a unique identifier instead of as a reachable address.


# 3.4.1 Algorithm Agility


> The two certificates SHOULD be equivalent except for cryptographic algorithm; i.e. the key usages SHOULD match.

This feels ambiguous to me and should be tightened up; specifically "equivalent certificates" is not a term that's defined in 5280, so I think we need a lot more words here to say what we mean. I suggest:

> The two certificates SHOULD be equivalent meaning that they describe the same subject for the same purpose; i.e. have the same Subject and Subject Alternative Names, and the same EKU and other extensions, allowing for differences resulting from the two certificates MAY be issued by different CAs and therefore would have different Issuer, revocation extensions, DN suffix, etc. In general, the more differences between the two cert, the more possibility that a client rejects them.

issues.title: what does "matching identifiers" mean?
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
```
3.4.3.  Dual Usage

   This purpose indicates the referenced certificate's purpose is for
   dual usage; i.e. the related certificates belong to the same entity
   and one provides a signing-type key while the other provides an
   encryption-type key.  The two certificates SHOULD have matching
   identifiers.
```

What does "matching identifiers" mean in that paragraph?

Is it that each should have an SIA extension with id-on-relatedCertificateDescriptor, which refer to each other?  This is problematic when using CertDiscoveryMethod.byInclusion.  The secondary certificate obviously can't include the primary certificate, and referencing the primary certificate by URI is pointless because the secondary certificate was just pulled out of the primary one.

What is the purpose of having matching identifiers? Are you intending to ensure that both certificates are owned by the same subject?

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
I think by "matching identifiers" we meant DN and SANs. IE it would be sorta meaningless for two certs to be "Dual Usage" they have completely different subjects.

We should replace the word "identifiers" with something less ambiguous.

issues.title: what to do with unknown DiscoveryPurposeId?
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
DiscoveryPurposeId is OPTIONAL, but I think you should still say what should be done if one comes across an unknown DiscoveryPurposeId.  Just behave as if there was no DiscoveryPurposeId?

If a DiscoveryPurposeId exists, does this mean that the secondary certificate can only be used in the way specified by the DiscoveryPurposeId?  If so, state that.  And then if the DiscoveryPurposeId is unknown, the secondary certificate MUST NOT be used?

comments.author: johngray-dev
comments.body:
The way we designed the draft, it should be possible to use discovery purposes that are not defined in the draft.  If someone comes up with a use-case for their environment that we didn't think about.  That is why we made it extensible.  

Ok, we will make the draft more clear.   To answer the questions:

If a DiscoveryPurposeId exists, does this mean that the secondary certificate can only be used in the way specified by the DiscoveryPurposeId? 

The purpose is meant as the intended usage, but we can't enforce how an application would use it.  We can add new language around that to make it more clear

We will look at the language in 5280 for EKU processing language to get inspiration on how to handle this. 
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc5280#section-4.2.1.12

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
The language in 5280 regardling EKU says:

~~~
   If the extension is present, then the certificate MUST only be used
   for one of the purposes indicated.  If multiple purposes are
   indicated the application need not recognize all purposes indicated,
   as long as the intended purpose is present.  Certificate using
   applications MAY require that the extended key usage extension be
   present and that a particular purpose be indicated in order for the
   certificate to be acceptable to that application.
~~~

It would be consistent to say that if an IntentID is unknown, then the corresponding data in the RCD SHALL NOT be used by the relying party. This avoids us having to come up with language that allows consumption of the RCD data as well as the corresponding issues that may arise due to mismatched Intents.

Thoughts?

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
I agree: mirroring the behaviour and language of EKUs makes sense to me.

A strawman example would be that someone registers an IntendID "Replaces" with the semantics that once you've seen the new cert, you should no longer accept the old cert. You would get yourself into trouble if you said "I don't recognize this IntentID, so I'll assume it's equivalent to Agility / Dual". Therefore "SHALL NOT process RCD's with an un-recognized IntentID" or "MUST ignore / skip RCDs with an un-recognized IntentID" makes sense to me.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body:
You say you want to mirror the behaviour and language of EKU, but then didn't like calling it DiscoveryPurposeId because it's too much like EKU (I got very confused scanning the github version and no longer finding DiscoveryPurposeId until I realized it was recently renamed).

I haven't thought much further, but I do like Purpose better than Intent because Intent implies an interpretation other than what the issuer specified but Purpose is more definitive, "this is what it is to be used for".

Anyways, I didn't come here to bikeshed, I had a thought about taking inspiration from EKU.  EKU allows multiple key usages.  Discovery intent currently doesn't allow multiple intents.  Should that be considered?  For example, a single referenced certificate could conceptually be used for agility or redundancy.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body: Another thought about multiple intents.  In the future one could add a Hybridization intent, where the Primary and Secondary keys are to be used together in a hybrid mode (up to the protocol to define how that hybridization works). But one might want to allow a certificate to be used with either Agility or Hybridization - if you sufficiently trust the secondary algorithm then use that, if you don't then it can be hybridized.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body: still going here... I realize that multiple RCDs could be used to achieve multiple intents, but that doesn't scale well when using byInclusion.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
The intent (pun not "intended") was that multiple RCDs are allowed in the SIA. I thought this was already explicit, but as you pointed out in the issue you created (thanks!) this should be clarified in the text.

As for bikeshed color: we renamed Purpose to Intent after someone said the name as too similar to KeyPurpose and several of the authors agreed. I personally liked Purpose and didn't see how it could be confused with KeyPurpose, but I was in the rough.

comments.author: johngray-dev
comments.body:
I liked PurposeID as well, but I think it was Russ that thought it might be confused with other IETF drafts around EKU.  See this issue:  https://github.com/lamps-wg/certificatediscovery/issues/24

Me can always put it back to PurposeID if the working group likes it better.

issues.title: accessLocation semantics not defined?
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
Section 3.5 "Signature Algorithm and Public Key Algorithm fields" says:

```
   The semantics of other id-ad-certDiscovery accessLocation name forms
   are not defined.

   Note: For a description of uniformResourceIdentifier consult section
   4.2.2.1 of [!RFC5280].
```

This seems like it should in be a separate section from one talking about the algorithm fields.

Also, if you aren't willing to define the semantics for the other accessLocation forms, why use GeneralName?  Just use IA5String instead.  GeneralName seems pretty disliked: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/spasm/rqZ5Vl8ryCdPvLN_l8uvizpDsA0/

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
> This seems like it should in be a separate section from one talking about the algorithm fields.

I agree, this language should be in another section.

We need to use `GeneralName` because that is the type of the `accessLocation` field (it's not ANY). The type of this `accessLocation` value will be the RCD AnotherName. I think we should strengthen the following:

```
The semantics of other id-ad-certDiscovery accessLocation name forms
   are not defined.
```

To make it a MUST NOT use any other name form.

issues.title: Primary certificate validation failure
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
Section 3.5 "Signature Algorithm and Public Key Algorithm fields" says:

```
   When the validation of the Primary Certificate fails, the software
   that understands the SIA extension and the certDiscovery access
   method uses the information to determine whether to fetch the
   Secondary Certificate.  The software will look at the
   signatureAlgorithm and publicKeyAlgorithm to determine whether the
   Secondary Certificate has the signature algorithm and certificate
   public key algorithm it can process.  If the software understands the
   signature algorithm and certificate public key algorithm, the
   software fetches the certificate from the URI specified in the
   relatedCertificateLocation and attempts another validation.
   Otherwise, the validation simply fails.
```
at a minimum this should probably say (note the MAY):
```
   the software
   that understands the SIA extension and the certDiscovery access
   method MAY use the information to determine whether to fetch the
   Secondary Certificate.
```
But really, if the primary certificate validation fails then you can't trust anything in the primary certificate, including any part of the RelatedCertificateDescriptor. As a minimum, the signature on the certificate needs to be valid in order to use the secondary certificate.  There could be other policy reasons why the primary certificate fails validation which would prevent the secondary certificate from being used.  Maybe if the primary certificate is expired or revoked, the secondard certificate could still be trusted if the signature verification succeeds.

The software may also want to check these algorithm fields when primary certificate validation succeeds, e.g. to avoid unnecessary fetches when it already knows it doesn't understand the algorithms.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body: I agree that there's some security issues here. Good point.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
If I'm understanding correctly, there's two things that need to be made explicit in the draft:

1. The primary certificate need not be validated to consume the RCD contained within. However, the process to retrieve and validate the Secondary Certificate needs to be done with caution, perhaps referencing the security considerations in 5280 for AIA CA-Issuers.
2. The signature and key algorithm fields can be used to "fail fast" so the relying party doesn't waste bandwidth and processing cycles downloading something it can't use. This is the intent behind the fields, but we should make it explicit.

@danvangeest @ounsworth @tomofumiokubo @mandelj7  @johngray-dev do we agree on the above? If so, I can create a PR.

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
> The signature and key algorithm fields can be used to "fail fast" so the relying party doesn't waste bandwidth and processing cycles downloading something it can't use. This is the intent behind the fields, but we should make it explicit.

This I'm 100% on board with.


> The primary certificate need not be validated to consume the RCD contained within.

This feels like a swamp full of alligators. "I couldn't establish trust in this cert, but it's cool cause it pointed me to that other cert claiming that it's equivalent". DANGER. That's how you get conference papers with pictures of Mallory wearing a Bob mask. Fetching AIA's is a bit different because that's a recursive validation: you go up the chain and then you come back down the chain to the same place you started so if the un-verified link was malicious, you'll catch that on the down-swing when the signature validation fails on the EE containing the malicious AIA; whereas here we're shunting you over to a new chain, there's no return-swing to catch the malicious link, right? This feels "Here Be Dragons". Unless we have a compelling reason to allow you to follow un-validated RCD's, then I would not make an exception for them.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
> Unless we have a compelling reason to allow you to follow un-validated RCD's, then I would not make an exception for them.

How do you expect the redundancy intent to work?

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
> This feels like a swamp full of alligators. "I couldn't establish trust in this cert, but it's cool cause it pointed me to that other cert claiming that it's equivalent".

How can it be that here we are talking about "equivalence" of certificates? This is something completely different than discovery. To "disover" someting means "finding" it. No more. Please make sure that this draft is clear in its title, abstract, and introduction regarding the intended purpose. If you want to realise more than mere certificate discovery, I see the need to change the title.

issues.title: Path processing of secondary certificate
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body: Is path processing of the secondary certificate just left as an implementation problem?  Where is the verifier supposed to get the issuer of the secondary certificate?  Must it be issued by the same issuer of the primary certificate?  If not, must the issuer of the secondary certificate be sent in the same certificate list as the issuer of the primary certificate?  Aren't there plenty of naïve path builders which assume that the certificate list is an ordered list of certificates from the EE to the root, and would bork on such an unexpected cert in the list?

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
Interesting point.
I believe you are blurring the line between the Cert Validation layer and the protocol layer -- like, I think "certificate list" is a CMS thing or a TLS thing, or a P12 thing, but not strictly an X.509 thing.

But you raise a good point.

I think we do not want the two certs to have to come from the some issuer.

You're right that we should put some text about this.

> When the two certificates have different Issuers, the application will need to be provided with both certificate chains; the details of conveying multiple certificate chains will vary by protocol. Implementers SHOULD consider whether their protocols and applications are capable of conveying multiple certificate chains when designing their Related Certificate deployment.

something like that?

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body: It should also probably say something like certificate path validation for the secondary certificate MUST follow section 6 of RFC 5280.  Although it's probably obvious, it should probably be stated so no one assumes that because it is extracted from a validated certificate that it's automatically validated.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
Unless I'm missing something, I think we already say this in the draft:
https://github.com/lamps-wg/certificatediscovery/blob/main/draft-ietf-lamps-certdiscovery.md?plain=1#L288.

Given this, I'm not sure if we need to make further changes.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body: @danvangeest do you think the blurb in the Security Considerations is sufficient, or should we clarify this further?

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body: Dan indirectly raises another good point: a Related Certificate IS NOT a delegated credential (aka "sub cert") [RFC9345]. You MAY NOT use the RDC extension to "cross-certify" the Related Certificate under the Primary CA. This would get absurdly complicated in the presence of hierarchical policy OIDs or name constraints. The Related Certificate has its own cert chain that MUST be validated independently in order for the Related Certificate to be considered valid.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body:
> [@danvangeest](https://github.com/danvangeest) do you think the blurb in the Security Considerations is sufficient, or should we clarify this further?

Security Considerations says this:

> Retrieval of the Secondary Certificate is not sufficient to consider the Secondary Certificate trustworthy. The certification path validation algorithm as defined in section 6 of {{RFC5280}} MUST be performed for the Secondary Certificate.

So that's certainly enough to address my comment from Feb 11.

I think Mike's suggested text from Feb 11 would address my initial concerns.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body:
Mike's suggested text:

> When the two certificates have different Issuers, the application will need to be provided with both certificate chains; the details of conveying multiple certificate chains will vary by protocol. Implementers SHOULD consider whether their protocols and applications are capable of conveying multiple certificate chains when designing their Related Certificate deployment.

I guess it's not just implementers which need to consider these things.  Protocol designers who integrate Certificate discovery into their protocols will need to consider it as well.  In particular they'll need to consider whether they need to extend their protocol to send a second certificate chain vs sending a single list, and if they send a single list they should consider the existence of naïve path builders.

RFC 8446 says:

> The sender's certificate MUST come in the first
   CertificateEntry in the list.  Each following certificate SHOULD
   directly certify the one immediately preceding it.

So this is evidence that sending a single list of certificates will not fit well into some protocols.

comments.author: falko-strenzke
comments.body:
> RFC 8446 says:
> 
> > The sender's certificate MUST come in the first
> > CertificateEntry in the list.  Each following certificate SHOULD
> > directly certify the one immediately preceding it.
> 
> So this is evidence that sending a single list of certificates will not fit well into some protocols.

This is exactly the point that I tried to raise in the session yesterday. In my view, the draft does not draw a clear line between mere certificate discovery and the modification of protocols to use alternate chains. The latter is clearly not covered by the "certificate discovery" function. The discovery aspect, in my understanding, is clearly limited to making known another certificate. That means very concretely that, if the peer already knows the given alternative certificate, the certificate discovery extension conveys no information to him at all. But from the discussion here I understand that the intend of the authors is indeed to cross over into the protocoll domain, i.e., provide the possibility for protocols to use the extension for use of alternative certificates in a single protocol run. If that is really the case, then the draft should explain the intended possible modification of protocols for this purpose in detail. The interaction with RFC 5280 then needs to be elaborated precisely. The title and abstract would also have to be modified.

issues.title: Link to a single ASN.1 module in all sections to prevent divergence
issues.author: johngray-dev
issues.body: When ASN.1 is used in multiple places in the draft and the module is at the bottom, it mean any changes need to be update in multiple places.  This is easy to miss which can result in errata down the road (this had happened to other RFC's recently).  To prevent this we should create a separate ASN.1 module and use markdown (or other magic) to pull in that data from a single source.

issues.title: How are these certificates requested?
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
RFC 9763 describes a PKCS#10 CSR attribute which is used to prove ownership of the related certificate before the RelatedCertificate extension can be added to the newly issued certificate.

Your draft is much more complicated than RFC 9763 and so should also address similar issuance problems.
- how does the requester indicate to the RA that it wants this type of certificate?
- how is the discovery method and purpose communicated by the requesting party to the RA? Just in a SIA in the CSR?  Say something about this. 
- how does the RA validate this information? What if certificates are cross-referenced by URL?  At least one of the URLs will point to nothing at time of issuance.
- does the requester have to prove posession of either of the keys?  Either way, say something in the security considerations.

comments.author: ralienpp
comments.body:
Some thoughts and data points, based on a prototype we built to get a feel of this approach.

> how does the requester indicate to the RA that it wants this type of certificate?

In our experiment we used HTTP for transport; we have an endpoint, say `/cert-discovery` - it is known in advance that if I send my request here, I'll get back a certificate that contains the discovery extension. Alternatively, one could use some reverse proxy that looks at custom HTTP headers and routes the request based on those. I never put too much thought into it, I assumed that the draft deliberately omitted such a discussion to keep the approach transport-agnostic.

> how is the discovery method and purpose communicated by the requesting party to the RA? Just in a SIA in the CSR?

Our test implementation (a mock CA and a CMP client) do it as you described - by looking for SIA inside `tbsCertificate.extensions`. We prototyped it with PKCS#10 because at the time we already had the needed primitives and it was a low-hanging fruit. But as above, my assumption was that the document does not go into the details, to avoid tying it to a specific format - this can work with CRMF too (in contrast with RFC 9763, which is only meant to be used in conjunction with PKCS#10).


> At least one of the URLs will point to nothing at time of issuance.

It is a possibility, and I see it as a feature. Imagine a spectrum ranging from "loosely coupled" to "tightly coupled". If I'm in an environment where there is no certainty at the moment when the certificate is issued (e.g., we haven't yet decided which PQ algorithm to use for the referenced certificate), then I put in the URL as a placeholder, knowing that it is not alive yet, but will be soon ("soon" can be defined in the policy of the CA).

Such loose coupling is weaker than having the hash of the other certificate (or embedding the other certificate) - but it is a deliberate business decision, it allows a bias for action - "release now, think thoroughly a bit later". Other approaches (like RFC 9763) imply that the other certificate must exist at the time we're issuing `this` certificate, so there is less room for maneuver.

> does the requester have to prove posession of either of the keys? Either way, say something in the security considerations.

My assumption was that the CA's policy will cover that gap - so the document is deliberately not trying to specify it.


------

As you can see, our implementation is based on some assumptions. I am unaware of other implementations, so there were no interop tests yet, and no way to check if at least one other person on the planet saw the text in the same way. I hope that my feedback will help others get a sense of how this might work in practice.

In any case, the fact that you raise these questions indicates that interpretations can differ. In my opinion, it would help if the text could explicitly state things like "we refrain from specifying it in greater detail to allow flexibility, but one way to do it would be X".

comments.author: ounsworth
comments.body:
Good question.

I think the answer to "What must the CA do in order to establish the validity of the RCD link?" is going to depend on what the IntentID is and other details of the issuance profile and CA policy. For example, if you have a cert profile that says if you enrol for a signing cert, the CA will also create you a server-generated KEM key and send you down a pair of Signing - KEM certs linked with RCD: Dual-Usage. Fine. The client didn't need to do anything for that to work securely.

But if you're doing `id-rcd-priv-key-stmt`, then the CA really should do some sort of check that both keys are simultaneously held by the same entity (and exactly what it should do is going to depend on whether they are both signing keys, both KEM keys, etc). Do you want to do PoP? Would the CA accept an Attested CSR [draft-ietf-lamps-csr-attestation] with a pair of Key Attestations [draft-ietf-rats-pkix-key-attestation] from the same HSM / TPM? etc etc etc.

I think Alex and I are in the same camp that the ambiguities are actually features to allow CAs to set their own policies appropriate to the specific problem they're trying to solve. I agree that this is worth explicitly stating in the draft so that it doesn't look like an omission.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body: I agree that just saying that it is a problem that this draft isn't trying to solve (for X reasons?) is good enough.

issues.title: Request for Cryptographic SPKI Binding and delta encoding (for size efficiency)
issues.author: johngray-dev
issues.body:
Sarat G is suggesting the following:

After working through some high-assurance use cases in the
embedded/hardware security space, I'd like to advocate for two
additions to Certificate Discovery:

1. A delta encoding option for byInclusion (size efficiency)
2. A mandatory binding attribute (SPKI hash) for high-assurance
   profiles

These are independent — the draft could adopt either without the
other.


USE CASE: IMMUTABLE HARDWARE ROOTS OF TRUST

In secure boot, a public key (or its hash) is burned into OTP fuses
at manufacturing. These devices have 15-20 year deployed lifetimes
(automotive ECUs, industrial controllers, HSMs). I've encountered a
critical need to graft modern cryptographic properties onto these
legacy anchors — specifically:

- Post-quantum algorithm adoption (ML-DSA, LMS) on hardware whose
  OTP root is ECDSA or RSA
- Threshold/MPC signing (e.g., FROST over Ed25519) on hardware whose
  OTP key type doesn't support efficient threshold protocols
- Key compromise recovery without hardware replacement

For these devices:
- byUri is not viable: no network stack during early-stage boot.
- byInclusion with a full certificate is often too large: early
  bootloaders operate under tight flash and MTU constraints. A delta
  encoding (carrying only the fields that differ) is significantly
  more compact.


USE CASE: ENDPOINT-DRIVEN POLICY ENFORCEMENT

In the scenarios above, the related certificate isn't just a
"discovery" convenience — it's a security requirement. The boot
chain must enforce that the paired descriptor is present and that its
secondary key satisfies the endpoint's policy (e.g., "firmware must
be signed under the threshold key, not just the legacy OTP key").

If the descriptor can be stripped or transplanted to a different
base certificate, an attacker can force a downgrade to the legacy
key alone. This is where binding matters.

In these high-assurance environments, the discovery mechanism must
act as a mandatory constraint: if the verifier's policy requires a
secondary key and it is missing or the binding is invalid, the
entire chain must be rejected.


SPKI BINDING (SOLVING THE CIRCULAR DEPENDENCY)

Your suggestion of considering SPKI binding in both directions is
exactly what I've been exploring. Since both public keys are
available before the signing ceremony:

- The related certificate (or descriptor) includes
  H(base.subjectPublicKeyInfo)
- The base certificate includes H(delta.subjectPublicKeyInfo)

This avoids the circular dependency (no certificate-level hash
needed) while cryptographically tying the two certificates together.
A verifier that sees a base certificate without the expected
descriptor, or with a descriptor whose binding doesn't match,
rejects the chain.

For standard PKI/TLS use, the binding could remain optional. For
high-assurance profiles (secure boot, code signing, IoT device
identity), it would be mandatory.


NEXT STEPS

I can draft a short text contribution with the ASN.1 for the binding
attribute if that would be useful. Happy to discuss further.

comments.author: sg777
comments.body:
# Proposed Text and ASN.1 for SPKI Binding and Delta Encoding

Thank you for creating this issue, John. Below is the proposed ASN.1
and supporting text for both additions.

## 1. SPKI Binding

### Problem

The current `certHash` in `CertLocation` hashes the full DER-encoded
Secondary Certificate. This works for `byUri` (hash-then-fetch), but
creates a circular dependency for bidirectional binding: you cannot
include H(CertB) inside CertA if CertA's hash must also appear inside
CertB — both certificates would need to be signed simultaneously.

Additionally, `certHash` does not prevent **descriptor transplantation**:
an attacker who obtains a valid Primary Certificate with a
`RelatedCertificateDescriptor` could transplant that descriptor into a
different certificate (e.g., by re-signing with a compromised CA),
causing a verifier to accept a binding between two unrelated
identities.

### Solution: SPKIBinding

Since both public keys are known before either certificate is signed,
we can bind using hashes of the `subjectPublicKeyInfo` (SPKI) fields
instead of full certificate hashes. This avoids the circular dependency
and enables bidirectional binding.

The `SPKIBinding` contains two components:

- **hostSPKIHash**: Hash of the SPKI of the certificate *containing*
  this descriptor. This prevents descriptor transplantation — if the
  descriptor is moved to a different certificate, the hostSPKIHash
  will not match.

- **relatedSPKIHash** (OPTIONAL): Hash of the SPKI of the related
  certificate. This cross-binding identifies the specific secondary
  certificate. When both certificates include descriptors pointing to
  each other, both with hostSPKIHash and relatedSPKIHash, the binding
  is bidirectional.

### Proposed ASN.1

Add a new optional field to `RelatedCertificateDescriptor`:

```asn1
RelatedCertificateDescriptor ::= SEQUENCE {
  method CertDiscoveryMethod,
  purpose DiscoveryPurposeId OPTIONAL,
  signatureAlgorithm [0] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
  publicKeyAlgorithm [1] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
  spkiBinding [2] IMPLICIT SPKIBinding OPTIONAL
}

SPKIBinding ::= SEQUENCE {
  hostSPKIHash CertHash,
  relatedSPKIHash CertHash OPTIONAL
}
```

`CertHash` is already defined in the draft (SEQUENCE of value +
hashAlgorithm). The same type is reused here.

### Verification Procedure

When a verifier processes a `RelatedCertificateDescriptor` that
contains an `spkiBinding`:

1. Compute H(Primary.subjectPublicKeyInfo) using the hash algorithm
   specified in `hostSPKIHash.hashAlgorithm`.
2. Compare with `hostSPKIHash.value`. If mismatch, REJECT (descriptor
   transplantation detected).
3. If `relatedSPKIHash` is present and the Secondary Certificate has
   been retrieved: compute H(Secondary.subjectPublicKeyInfo) and
   compare with `relatedSPKIHash.value`. If mismatch, REJECT.

For bidirectional binding, both certificates contain descriptors
pointing to each other. The verifier performs the above check on both
descriptors.

### When to Require Binding

For standard PKI/TLS discovery use cases, `spkiBinding` is OPTIONAL.
Existing implementations that do not understand the field ignore it.

For high-assurance profiles (secure boot, code signing, IoT device
identity), the verifier's local policy SHOULD require that
`spkiBinding` is present and valid. If the verifier's policy mandates
binding and the field is absent, the verifier MUST reject the
descriptor. This transforms the discovery mechanism from an optional
convenience into a mandatory constraint.

A new purpose identifier could be registered to signal this intent:

```asn1
id-rcd-mandatory OBJECT IDENTIFIER ::= { id-rcd 6 }
```

When `purpose` is `id-rcd-mandatory`, the consuming application MUST
verify the `spkiBinding` and MUST reject the certificate chain if the
binding is absent or invalid.


## 2. Delta Encoding for byInclusion

### Problem

`byInclusion` embeds the full DER-encoded Secondary Certificate inside
the Primary Certificate's `subjectInfoAccess` extension. For use cases
where the Primary and Secondary certificates share most fields (same
subject, same issuer, same validity, same extensions — differing only
in key type and signature algorithm), this duplicates significant data.

In constrained environments (secure boot, IoT, embedded systems with
limited flash/NVM), this duplication is costly. A delta encoding that
carries only the fields that differ is substantially more compact.

### Solution: byDelta

Add a new `CertDiscoveryMethod` choice that carries a delta descriptor
— only the fields that differ between Primary and Secondary
certificates. The verifier reconstructs the full Secondary Certificate
by starting from the Primary Certificate's TBS and replacing the
fields specified in the delta.

### Proposed ASN.1

```asn1
CertDiscoveryMethod ::= CHOICE {
  byUri [0] IMPLICIT CertLocation,
  byInclusion Certificate,
  byLocalPolicy NULL,
  byDelta [1] IMPLICIT DeltaCertDescriptor
}

DeltaCertDescriptor ::= SEQUENCE {
  serialNumber CertificateSerialNumber,
  signature [0] IMPLICIT AlgorithmIdentifier OPTIONAL,
  issuer [1] IMPLICIT Name OPTIONAL,
  validity [2] IMPLICIT Validity OPTIONAL,
  subject [3] IMPLICIT Name OPTIONAL,
  subjectPublicKeyInfo SubjectPublicKeyInfo,
  extensions [4] IMPLICIT Extensions OPTIONAL,
  signatureValue BIT STRING
}
```

This structure is derived from the Delta Certificate Descriptor
defined in draft-bonnell-lamps-chameleon-certs-07. The required fields
are:

- **serialNumber**: The Secondary Certificate's serial number (always
  differs).
- **subjectPublicKeyInfo**: The Secondary Certificate's public key
  (always differs — the primary purpose of having two certificates).
- **signatureValue**: The Secondary Certificate's signature (always
  differs — signed by the issuer over the secondary TBS).

Optional fields (signature algorithm, issuer, validity, subject,
extensions) are included only when they differ from the Primary
Certificate. When absent, the Primary Certificate's values are used
during reconstruction.

### Reconstruction Procedure

To reconstruct the full Secondary Certificate from the Primary
Certificate and a `DeltaCertDescriptor`:

1. Start with the Primary Certificate's TBSCertificate.
2. Remove the `subjectInfoAccess` extension containing this descriptor.
3. Replace `serialNumber` with `delta.serialNumber`.
4. Replace `subjectPublicKeyInfo` with `delta.subjectPublicKeyInfo`.
5. If `delta.signature` is present, replace `signature` (TBS) and
   `signatureAlgorithm` (outer).
6. If `delta.issuer` is present, replace `issuer`.
7. If `delta.validity` is present, replace `validity`.
8. If `delta.subject` is present, replace `subject`.
9. If `delta.extensions` is present, replace matching extensions
   (by OID).
10. Set the outer `signatureValue` to `delta.signatureValue`.

The result is a complete, verifiable X.509 Certificate.

### Size Comparison (Example)

For an ECDSA P-256 Primary + Ed25519 Secondary with identical
subject/issuer/validity:

| Method | Overhead |
|--------|----------|
| byInclusion (full cert) | ~600-800 bytes |
| byDelta (serial + SPKI + sig only) | ~150-180 bytes |

For PQC scenarios (ECDSA Primary + ML-DSA-65 Secondary):

| Method | Overhead |
|--------|----------|
| byInclusion (full cert) | ~3500-4000 bytes |
| byDelta | ~2800-3200 bytes |

The savings are most significant when Primary and Secondary share all
fields except key and signature.


## Combined Example

A high-assurance Primary Certificate (ECDSA P-256) with a delta-encoded
Secondary Certificate (Ed25519) and mandatory SPKI binding:

```
Primary Certificate
  tbsCertificate:
    subjectPublicKeyInfo: ECDSA P-256
    extensions:
      subjectInfoAccess:
        accessMethod: id-ad-certDiscovery
        accessLocation: otherName
          type-id: id-on-relatedCertificateDescriptor
          value: RelatedCertificateDescriptor
            method: byDelta
              serialNumber: <secondary serial>
              signature: Ed25519 (1.3.101.112)
              subjectPublicKeyInfo:
                algorithm: Ed25519
                subjectPublicKey: <32 bytes>
              signatureValue: <64 bytes>
            purpose: id-rcd-mandatory
            publicKeyAlgorithm: Ed25519
            spkiBinding:
              hostSPKIHash:
                value: H(Primary.SPKI)
                hashAlgorithm: SHA-256
              relatedSPKIHash:
                value: H(Secondary.SPKI)
                hashAlgorithm: SHA-256
```

The verifier:
1. Validates the Primary Certificate normally.
2. Checks `spkiBinding.hostSPKIHash` against computed H(Primary.SPKI).
3. Reconstructs the Secondary Certificate from the delta.
4. Checks `spkiBinding.relatedSPKIHash` against computed
   H(Secondary.SPKI).
5. Validates the reconstructed Secondary Certificate.
6. Since `purpose` is `id-rcd-mandatory`, enforces that the secondary
   key satisfies local policy.

## 3. Security Considerations

### Why SPKI Binding Instead of Certificate Binding

A natural alternative to SPKI binding is full certificate binding:
include H(Secondary Certificate) in the Primary and H(Primary
Certificate) in the Secondary. This creates a circular dependency —
the hash of CertA depends on the contents of CertA, which includes
the hash of CertB, which depends on the contents of CertB, which
includes the hash of CertA. Neither certificate can be signed first.

The existing `certHash` in `CertLocation` avoids this by being
unidirectional: only the Primary includes H(Secondary). This works
for discovery but does not provide bidirectional binding.

SPKI binding resolves the circularity because
`subjectPublicKeyInfo` is determined before signing. Both public
keys are generated (and known) before either certificate's TBS is
assembled. Therefore H(Primary.SPKI) and H(Secondary.SPKI) can be
computed and embedded in both certificates' descriptors without
creating a dependency cycle.

### Descriptor Transplantation Attack

Without `hostSPKIHash`, an attacker who possesses two valid
certificates from the same CA — CertA (ECDSA) and CertB (ECDSA,
different key) — could transplant CertA's `RelatedCertificateDescriptor`
(containing a PQC delta) onto CertB. A verifier would then associate
the PQC identity with CertB's key, which the attacker controls.

`hostSPKIHash` prevents this: the descriptor contains
H(CertA.SPKI). When transplanted to CertB, the verifier computes
H(CertB.SPKI), finds a mismatch, and rejects the descriptor. The
binding is non-fungible — it cannot be moved between certificates.

### Descriptor Stripping Attack

An attacker who controls the certificate delivery channel (e.g.,
a TLS middlebox or a compromised firmware delivery pipeline) could
strip the `subjectInfoAccess` extension entirely, forcing the
verifier to accept only the Primary (classical) certificate.

For standard PKI use, this is acceptable — the Primary certificate
is still valid. For high-assurance profiles where the secondary key
is a security requirement (e.g., threshold signing in secure boot),
stripping constitutes a downgrade attack.

The `id-rcd-mandatory` purpose identifier addresses this: when a
verifier's local policy requires the secondary key and the descriptor
is absent, the verifier MUST reject the certificate chain. This
converts stripping from an invisible downgrade into a detectable
failure.

### Delta Reconstruction and DER Determinism

When using `byDelta`, the verifier reconstructs the Secondary
Certificate by modifying the Primary Certificate's TBS and applying
the delta fields. To ensure signature verification succeeds, the
reconstruction MUST produce a byte-for-byte identical TBS to the
one the CA signed. This requires:

1. All certificates and delta descriptors MUST use DER (Distinguished
   Encoding Rules), not BER. DER guarantees a single canonical
   encoding for any ASN.1 value.
2. The reconstruction procedure MUST remove the `subjectInfoAccess`
   extension entry containing the delta descriptor before
   re-encoding the TBS. Failure to remove it would alter the TBS
   and cause signature verification to fail.

### Hash Algorithm Agility

The `CertHash` type used for SPKI binding includes a
`hashAlgorithm` field. All implementations MUST support SHA-256.
For environments requiring post-quantum hash resilience, SHA-3
(id-sha3-256) or SHAKE256 MAY be used. The hash algorithm for the
binding is independent of the certificate's signature algorithm —
a certificate signed with ML-DSA can use SHA-256 for its SPKI
binding.

### Multi-Hop Chains

In a certificate chain with multiple graft points (e.g., Root →
Intermediate → Leaf, where both Root and Intermediate carry
descriptors), each descriptor's `spkiBinding` is verified
independently at its own level. The binding at level N does not
depend on the binding at level N-1. This ensures that compromise
or removal of a descriptor at one level does not affect the
binding integrity at other levels.

Verifiers processing multi-hop chains SHOULD verify all descriptors
in the chain before accepting the secondary trust path. This enables
transitive bidirectional trust: the endpoint can verify the binding
at every level of the hierarchy, ensuring the security policy is
enforced from the leaf back to the root.

---

Happy to refine any of this based on feedback.

Regards,
Sarat G

issues.title: Clarify the Redundancy intent
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
> This intent indicates the referenced certificate's intent is to provide operational redundancy; i.e. the Secondary Certificate could be issued by a different CA or has a different validity period which can be used as a backup if the Primary set of certificates is about to expire.

What is the Primary **set** of certificates?  Should this instead say "... if the Primary Certificate is about to expire"

comments.author: mandelj7
comments.body: @danvangeest yes, you are correct. Need to take out "set of". Thanks.

issues.title: Statement of Posession intent and revocation
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
The *Statement of Possession of a Private Key* section says:

> The reason for carrying a RelatedCertificateDescriptor of this type is to track that the Primary Certificate had a trust dependency on the Secondary Certificate at the time of issuance and that presumably the two private keys are co-located on the same key storage. Therefore if one certificate is revoked, they SHOULD both be revoked.

I don't know if I agree with the "SHOULD be revoked".  All the statement of possession is doing is indicating that the subject had access to the signing key of the Secondary Certificate at the time of the Primary certificate's issuance. If the secondary certificate's key is compromised and revoked at a later time, the only potential thing linking them is that the private keys may have been co-located on storage.  But also they might not be co-located. Really it should be a policy of the CA, if one private key is compromised then all co-located private keys should be revoked, but this has nothing to do with the certificate discovery extensions.

issues.title: Make it explicit that multiple discovery access methods are allowed
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
Suggestion to make it explicit that multiple discovery access methods are allowed.  RFC 5280 does the same thing for some accessMethods (but not all of them, without forbidding multiples - why?).

OLD:

> This document defines a new access method `id-ad-certDiscovery` which is an OBJECT IDENTIFIER that indicates the `accessMethod` is for certificate discovery.

NEW:

> This document defines a new access method `id-ad-certDiscovery` which is an OBJECT IDENTIFIER that indicates the `accessMethod` is for certificate discovery.
  A subjectInfoAccess extension may include multiple instances of the
   id-ad-certDiscovery accessMethod.  The different instances may specify
   different discovery methods or may specify different intents for the same
   method.

issues.title: Self Reference Intent and binding subjects
issues.author: danvangeest
issues.body:
The Self Reference intent says:

> This intent can be used to bind the subjects of Primary and Secondary Certificates. The Primary Certificate contains a self-reference to its location, as well as a reference to the Secondary Certificate. The Secondary Certificate contains a self-reference to its location, and a reference to the Primary Certificate. Provided that policy requires subject equivalence when this mechanism is used, then the consuming application can treat both certificates as certifying the same entity.

I don't understand how the self-reference is helping with this binding.  Isn't the Primary->Secondary and Secondary->Primary cross-referencing achieving this?

Also for the cross-reference part when binding the subjects, is the Self Reference intent supposed to be used for this?  If so, that contradicts the previous paragraph:

> This intent indicates the Uniform Resource Identifier where this certificate is located. Applications which retrieve this certificate can then compare the retrieved certificate with this value to ensure that the correct certificate was retrieved.

assuming "this certificate" is the certificate containing the reference to its actual self.

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
> I don't understand how the self-reference is helping with this binding. Isn't the Primary->Secondary and Secondary->Primary cross-referencing achieving this?

The Self Reference Intent allows for a stronger binding. Generally, certificates will be retrieved using plaintext protocols (HTTP mainly) so it is possible that an on-path attacker could substitute the certificate. However, with the Self-reference, if the Primary and Secondary certificates are both trusted, then it is possible to ensure that the correct certificate was retrieved by comparing the requested URL with the Self Reference URL. This same mechanism is used with scoped CRLs and the names/URLs included in the CRL IDP and certificate's CRLDP to ensure the certificate is within the scope of the scoped CRL.

It's not as strong of a binding as a hash, but it allows for a two-way binding that the RP can use to ensure the correct certificate was retrieved.

comments.author: danvangeest
comments.body:
Is the mechanism for scoped CRLs described in an RFC somewhere?

Perhaps this Self Reference mechanism should be fleshed out more in this document instead of assuming people will know how to do it or will already be familiar with the scoped CRLs mechanism.

pulls.title: Update draft-lamps-okubo-certdiscovery.md

comments.author: johngray-dev
comments.body: Joe and John agreed to merge since it is just fixing broken ASN.1

comments.author: CBonnell
comments.body:
```
-----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
MIIDNDCCAhygAwIBAgIUDstUy84jHbn0oq14AI2eNxYGT9UwDQYJKoZIhvcNAQELBQAwIzEhMB8GA1UE
AwwYRXhhbXBsZSBTZWxmLVNpZ25lZCBSb290MB4XDTI2MDEyNDE3MDAzMloXDTI3MDEyNDE3MDAzMlow
IzEhMB8GA1UEAwwYRXhhbXBsZSBTZWxmLVNpZ25lZCBSb290MIIBIjANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQEFAAOCAQ8A
MIIBCgKCAQEAukoGF+GvhWO35e/hjx3hsSFnZu51sLNpV7cGkFG6+kFZ5aHP5mha3dZ5afzek74BALUQ
34S+Ux5augSuTx0mivQQ605t8bfEuTARSszQd/l9qUibABcZlzA2FYug8Vj6j5dfkl+uq90QkMKHJhuA
hwIVvcV0vdyv273bfMoCUosLTn5wc2DDdjXv4sNPLJYLAbjAkT3R2wKU2LSA8hOlvT6GQK83YE92rddU
rBFMST34boajVjabRJo+SGcfSj61+KQULNXYy7+s1jhGnL3/Kh5hH3SKjEXCQuu+yqYIZCy94Kajjhrv
w8tnTrFRPUFEbm0mLvSa244la3Jh+/xYRQIDAQABo2AwXjBcBggrBgEFBQcBCwRQME4wTAYJKwYBBQUH
```
